Off Calendar Apr-14-2025 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (Amended)
Off Calendar Nov-21-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF GREGORY WRIGHT'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Gregory Wright's motion for summary adjudication on the first cause of action is granted.

Plaintiff Gregory Wright entered into a partnership with Defendant Nasri George Samara and others by which they would work together in buying, fixing and selling cars. Samara later executed a promissory note, pledging to repay the principal amount Wright had loaned Samara. Samara failed to make any payments to Wright, and Wright brought an action against Samara. Wright now brings this motion for summary adjudication as to the first cause of action, breach of promissory note.

To prevail on summary adjudication, a moving plaintiff must prove all of the elements of the plaintiff's cause of action and must show that there is no complete defense. (CCP 437c(p)(1).) If the plaintiff meets this initial burden, the burden shifts to the defendant to show a triable issue of material fact exists. A triable issue of a material fact exists only if "the evidence would allow a reasonable trier of fact to find the underlying fact in favor of the party opposing the motion in accordance with the applicable standard of proof." (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 845.)

"A cause of action for nonpayment on a promissory note is one for breach of contract." (Poseidon Development, Inc. v. Woodland Lane Estates, LLC (2007) 152 Cal. App. 4th 1106, 1112.) In a simple action on a promissory note, Wright can prove the elements by (1) alleging the existence of the contract, (2) incorporating a copy of the note, and (3) demonstrating nonpayment in accordance with the note's terms. (FPI Development, Inc. v. Nakashima (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 367, 383.) It is undisputed that Samara signed the note. (SSUMF, 1.) It is undisputed that the note attached to Wright's declaration as Exhibit B is genuine. (SSUMF, 2-6.) It is undisputed that Wright has not received any payments from Samara. (SSUMF, 7.)

The burden now shifts to Samara to show that a triable issue exists as to an element of the cause of action or to a defense. Samara fails to raise a triable issue as to his affirmative defense of duress. Samara presents no evidence of any wrongful act or threat to pressure him into the promissory note, no evidence that he was afraid or intimidated, and no evidence that he would not have agreed to the promissory note absent such non-existent wrongful act or threat. Samara fails to raise a triable issue as to his affirmative defense of undue influence. Samara alleges that his father was ill and Wright orally promised him that amendments to the promissory note would be possible after signing. But this is not enough to rise to the level of undue influence. Finally, Samara fails to raise a triable issue of fact as to his affirmative defense of breach of contract. There is no evidence of a written or oral modification to the promissory note. As Samara has failed to show a triable issue of fact exists, the motion for summary adjudication on the first cause of action is granted.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: S. Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)

DEMURRER to Amended CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-27-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT GREGORY WRIGHT's DEMURRER to Amended CROSS COMPLAINT.

Gregory Wright's demurrers to the first amended cross-complaint (FACC) are sustained in part and overruled in part.

The FACC's misrepresentation counts (3, 4, 7) are not pled with the required particularity. (Stansfield v. Starkey (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 59, 73 ["how, when, where, to whom, and by what means the representations were tendered"].) Demurrers sustained with leave to amend.

The same facts can support negligence and fraud claims, as a complaint "may plead in the alternative." (Adams v. Paul (1995) 11 Cal.4th 583, 593.) Demurrer overruled.

order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)
